coredata.dc:description: Efficient hydrogen generation from water splitting is a key component of the hydrogen economy. It has been extensively researched for decades how electrochemically splitting water using electrocatalysts might provide a sustainable and environmentally friendly hydrogen energy source. Sluggish kinetics of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) hinders the process of overall water splitting. Although metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are attractive for generation of effective OER electrocatalysts, their activity is significantly hindered by their inherent lower conductivity. Here, we demonstrate a Zr-MOF-based composite with carbon dots (CDs) in order to increase their OER activity. Its exceptional morphology with higher porosity and greater surface area results in enhanced electrochemical activity. It reveals tremendously low onset potential, i.e., 1.40 V vs. RHE, and a remarkably small overpotential of 1.45 V vs. RHE to attain benchmark current density. It exhibited a minimal Tafel value of 37 mV/dec, conquering state-of-the-art catalysts for OER. The fabricated electrocatalyst demonstrated a lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 0.248 Ω, with exceptional durability for about 20 h in chronoamperometric studies and for up to 1500 CV cycles. All these results demonstrated that as-fabricated Zr-MOF-based composite is a probable and potential candidate for OER.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient hydrogen generation from water splitting is a key component of the hydrogen economy. It has been extensively researched for decades how electrochemically splitting water using electrocatalysts might provide a sustainable and environmentally friendly hydrogen energy source. Sluggish kinetics of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) hinders the process of overall water splitting. Although metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are attractive for generation of effective OER electrocatalysts, their activity is significantly hindered by their inherent lower conductivity. Here, we demonstrate a Zr-MOF-based composite with carbon dots (CDs) in order to increase their OER activity. Its exceptional morphology with higher porosity and greater surface area results in enhanced electrochemical activity. It reveals tremendously low onset potential, i.e., 1.40 V vs. RHE, and a remarkably small overpotential of 1.45 V vs. RHE to attain benchmark current density. It exhibited a minimal Tafel value of 37 mV/dec, conquering state-of-the-art catalysts for OER. The fabricated electrocatalyst demonstrated a lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 0.248 Ω, with exceptional durability for about 20 h in chronoamperometric studies and for up to 1500 CV cycles. All these results demonstrated that as-fabricated Zr-MOF-based composite is a probable and potential candidate for OER.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets generated during and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid depletion of fossil fuels, environmental concerns, and ongoing energy challenges necessitate the utilization of new clean and recyclable energy sources such as metal-air batteries, fuel cells, and hydrogen energy [1–3]. Research on environmentally benign and sustainable production of hydrogen energy through electrochemical conversion of water using electrocatalysts has been conducted for many years [4,5]. Hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and OER (Oxygen evolution reaction) are two half-reactions that are often involved in electrochemical water splitting [6,7]. Owing to its four “electron-proton” transfer steps, OER, which is bottleneck of water splitting, needs an elevated thermodynamic potential (beyond 1.23 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE)) [8–10]. To overcome its slow kinetics, it has been demonstrated that noble metal-based catalysts, particularly IrO2 and RuO2, are demonstrating effective electrocatalysts for OER [11,12]. Unfortunately, their limited stability, high cost, and rarity make them unsuitable for general use [13]. Thus, development of inexpensive noble metal-free materials as substitute OER electrocatalysts is extremely desirable [14].

Electrocatalysts, owing to their numerous intrinsic molecular metal sites, huge surface area, extremely porous structure, and changeable surface chemical groups, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), have sparked intense curiosity in a wide range of scientific domains [15–17]. Numerous studies on creation of MOFs as electrocatalysts have been conducted over past 10 years, focusing on two main approaches. First, to achieve higher conductivity and semiconductive properties in MOFs, orbitals of the molecular components must be appropriately overlapped [18–20]. Second, we synthesize redox-active MOFs from materials that facilitate the transfer of both electron and hole charges [21,22]. The main objective is to minimize energy barrier since it allows for easy electron transit in conjunction with space [23,24]. Similarly, unique applications of MOFs in several fields, including catalysis, have been made possible by their permeability and the synergistic impact of their MOF-based structure. I agree with the hypothesis that MOFs' structures could be designed to ascribe their active sites via combining a variety of metal elements alongside active linkers and that such structures can acquire amazing synergistic effects that significantly boost their catalytic capabilities. On other hand, MOFs may be used as building blocks to make a range of compounds generated from metals and carbons by closely examining elemental structures and compositions. While several MOF base materials have been created to catalyze OER [25–28].

For instance, L. Ai et al. prepared CoxCe1-x-BTC MOFs by a simple solvothermal approach. Prepared electrode demonstrated excellent electrochemical activity by exhibiting lower overpotential of 308 mV and smaller Tafel value of 107 mV/dec with durability of 25 h for OER [29]. MXene@Ce-MOF was successfully fabricated by X. Le et al. and evaluated for OER. Lower overpotential of 270 mV and minimal Tafel value of 163.8 mV/dec was demonstrated by fabricated material [30]. There are still some crucial problems that need to be resolved for electrocatalyst. For instance, because of insulating qualities of organic ligands and inadequate conjugation of metal-organic link, the majority of MOFs suffer from intrinsically low electroconductivity [31,32]. Composite formation of MOFs with carbon-based materials can be used to solve this problem. Along with MOF's, carbon dots (CDs) have proved their mark in various fields. Their stunning biocompatibility, slightest toxicity, outstanding optical properties and dazzling electrochemiluminescence have put them forward one of the finest hot spots for investigations in diversity of fields whether it is biosensing, catalysis, bioimaging, drug loading, drug delivery, and for transportation of genes [33–35]. For instance, CDs@MOF was evaluated for OER by M. Y. ur Rehman et al. Nano-composite exhibiting extraordinary OER activity by demonstrating lower Tafel value of 62 mV/dec, smaller overpotential of 320 mV and long-term stability of 40 h [36].

Zirconium (Zr) belongs to family of transition metal, like other transition metals, it is also ductile, malleable, makes stable compounds, offering extreme resistance to corrosion. When MOFs are fabricated using Zr as metal ions linked with organic moiety, result is a superb MOF with unparalleled stability under sever conditions of pressure and temperature. It even persisted to remain stable under drastic basic or acidic environment [37–39]. Zr-MOFs thus synthesized were able to be tested on enormous scales and have potential to surpass other contestants not only in efficiency but also in stability as well. This advancement witnessed accompanying of a new era for exploitation of brilliant potential of Zr-MOFs.

Herein present work, we synthesized MOFs consisting of PBDC (biphenyl dicarboxylate) and BDC (1,5-benzenedicarboxylicacid) as organic linkers with Zr as the metal ions. These MOFs were then used to make the composites with CDs. The synthesized MOFs and their composites were then used for OER and they expressed analogous efficiency with state-of-the-art electrocatalysts produced from noble metals because of the synergistic effect between CDs and MOF. The innovative aspect of this paper lies in the way it combines MOf and CDs to increase OER activity. They demonstrated remarkable stability, and it is anticipated that with further modifications, these composites will achieve even better performance in the future.

Analytical grade HNO3, acetone, Sulphuric Acid (H2SO4), ethanol, biphenyldicarboxylate (BPDC), 1,5-benzenedicarboxylicacid (BDC), Zirconium oxydichloried [ZrOCl2] and 4,4′-NN′-dimethylformamide (DMF) were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Without any further purification, these chemicals were used.

Li Wang et al. exploited the green chemistry and designed a plan to engineer carbon dots (CDs). This technique was devoid of any chemicals [40]. The sole precursor utilized for this synthesis was milk. This method presents no discernible hazards to human health or the environment, as it does not involve the use of hazardous chemicals. Thus, it can be considered a benign technique. This procedure involved mixing of ultra-pure water (20 mL) into natural milk (25 mL) in a clean beaker. Intense stirring continued for 15 min during the mixing process. A stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon cup having a capacity of 50 mL. In order to have minimum impurity and maximum purity, standard protocol of this procedure was followed. The mixture was transported into the thermal reactor which was then put in the electric furnace at 180 °C for 2 h. When the time period for the synthesis of CDs was completed and the oven gained the room temperature gradually, the reaction mixture from the auto clave was taken out. The centrifugation of the resultant material was then carried out at 13000 rpm. Upon completion of centrifugation, brown precipitates were obtained as the final product. These brown precipitates were then cleaned with abundance of milli-Q water. This process was preceded by cleaning with that of ethanol. These centrifuged and cleaned precipitates were kept in electric furnace for 18 h. The temperature of the oven was set at 50 °C. After drying, CDs were obtained as brown precipitates which were finely ground. In desiccator, these fine brown CDs were stored for further characterization and then for applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Manufacturing of metal organic framework (Zr-BDC) and CDs@Zr-BDC
ce:section.ce:para: With vigorous stirring, 1 mmol of BDC (0.166 g) was mixed in 10 mL of DMF. 10 mL of HCOOH was also mixed with the solution of BDC in DMF, but with continuous stirring. In this mixture, 1 mmol of ZrOCl2 (0.322 g) was also mixed, and the mixture was mixed continuously until a clear solution was collected. This solution was then transferred into a clean autoclave, which was then placed into an oven that was set for 3 days at 100 °C. When the stipulated time period was completed and temperature fell naturally to room temperature, further DMF was mixed into the solution in the autoclave, and it was agitated again. After vigorous stirring for some time, filtration of the solution was carried out. The resulting product was dried at room temperature. It was kept in desiccator. Further characterization and application of the synthesized material were carried out later on. With vigorous stirring, 1 mmol of BDC (0.166 g) was mixed in 10 ml of DMF, and then 10 mL of HCOOH was also mixed with the above solution with continual stirring. After that, 1 mmol of ZrOCl2 (0.322 g) was mixed in the mixture. This mixture was stirred continuously till a transparent solution was obtained. With continuous stirring, 0.161 g of CDs were also mixed at this stage into the solution. The above-mentioned procedure was followed for advanced synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of metal organic framework (Zr-BPDC-MOF) and CDS@Zr-BPDC
ce:section.ce:para: With vigorous stirring, 1.5 mmol of BPDC (0.363 g) was mixed in 20 ml of DMF. After that, 20 ml of HCOOH was also mixed with the solution of BPDC in DMF with continuous stirring. In this mixture, 1.5 mmol of ZrOCl2 (0.483 g) was mixed, and then the solution was stirred continuously till a transparent solution was collected. This mixture was then transferred to a clean autoclave and subsequently placed into an oven for 3 days at 100 °C. After specified time period had passed and temperature fell naturally to room temperature, further DMF was added to the solution mixture in the autoclave, and it was mixed again. After vigorous stirring for some time, filtration of the solution was carried out. Resulting material was dried at normal temperature. It was kept in desiccator. Further characterization and application of the synthesized material were carried out later on. The same method was used for the preparation of the nanocomposite, with the addition of already synthesized CDs (0.5 g).

For assessment of electrocatalytic activity of synthesized materials, their homogeneous inks were arranged by dissolving 15 mg of fabricated products (each) into 100 μL of ultra-pure water with repeated and intense stirring. The sonication of the solution was carried out for 60 min. We obtained a homogeneous ink for further evaluation of the OER. Then it was dropped on nickel foam (NF), prepared by using 1 × 1 cm pieces of NF washed with acetone and sonicated for 15 min. Same procedure was repeated with HCl (2.0 M), milli-Q water, and ethanol with continuous sonication. On the other hand, the modified NF pieces were placed in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

Powder x-ray diffraction (XRD) was utilized to study whether our synthesized material is crystalline or amorphous. It further investigated the crystallite sizes of the synthesized materials. A Bruker Diffractometer (D8-advance) consisting of Cu-Kα, radiation, and Ni-filter was used to bring about the XRD spectra of manufactured MOFs and their composites. For detection of functional entities that give all the characteristic properties to materials under examination, a Nicolet 170SXFTIR spectrometer (with a range of wave numbers of 400–4000 cm−1) was used for Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) elaborates on the morphology of manufactured materials, and equipped with Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (JSM5910) to investigate the elemental analysis. In this study, SEM (JSM5910) with a voltage of 20 kV was employed. The information about the surface area, pore size, and volume was provided by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), which was carried out through NOVA2200e for this study. Particle size and surface topology were further calculated by using an atomic force microscope (AFM, Agilent 5500).

AUTOLAB Potentiostate (PGSTAT-204) controlled by computer was used to carry out electrochemical studies of the fabricated MOFs and their composites. There are three electrodes with this potentiostate (PGSTAT-204). It has a cell made up of Teflon-covered Pyrex glass. The counter electrode used in this procedure was a wire made up of Pt. Ag/AgCl with a saturated solution of KCl as the reference electrode. NF, upon which the fabricated material was coated, served as the working electrode. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV), Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), and Chronoamperometry (CA) were the techniques for the assessment of intrinsic electrocatalytic potential of fabricated materials for OER. Cleaning and washing of cell were done prior to use, standard procedure was followed to diminish the chance of any contamination. In this procedure, cells were boiled in a solution of H2SO4 and HNO3 with a ratio of 1:3. It was then boiled in de-ionized water (DI). After boiling, it was rinsed with sufficient de-ionized water and acetone. For some time, prior to use, cell was put in electric furnace at 80 °C. Pt wire was also cleaned and washed using 20 % solution of nitric acid (HNO3). Adequate de-ionized water was then used to clean and wash the Pt wire. KOH (1 M) concentration was the electrolyte used in this research. The KOH solution, having a pH of 13.6 was used. All the electrochemical investigations were carried out at ambient temperature (298 K). Argon gas was used to purge the mixture 25 min prior to use. When Cyclic Voltammetric measurement was carried out, both potentials, i.e., +ve and −ve, were used with a sweeping speed of 5 mV s−1. Only +ve potential was employed when LSV was taken. At 100 mV s−1, various scans were recorded before the desired CV plots of the synthesized materials to minimize the risk of any error. For calculation purposes, only the submerged portion of working electrode was accounted for.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, structural, elemental and textural analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The solvothermal method using dimethylformamide (DMF) as the solvent was used to synthesize four different combinations of Zr-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and their associated hybrids with carbon dots (CDs). There were four possible configurations, named Zr-BDC, CDs@Zr-BDC, Zr-BPDC, and CDs@Zr-BDC. After production, these materials were characterized using several techniques, including SEM, AFM, XRD, FTIR, EDX, and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis. Zr-based MOFs and their hybrids with carbon dots (CSs) were characterized for size and shape using SEM. Morphological properties of Zr-BDC and CDs@Zr-BDC are shown in Fig. 1 (a–b). Zr-BDC appeared homogenous in its micrograph, with a rod-like cuboidal structure. The MOF particle size is determined using image-j software through a histogram given below in Fig. 1(e–i). In addition, the nanoparticle sizes of Zr-BDC, CDs@Zr-BDC, Zr-BPDC, and CDs@Zr-BPDC are 71, 77, 66, and 76 nm, respectively. Chemical alteration is likely responsible for the composite structure seen in this SEM scan, which shows a consistent distribution of carbon dots over the MOF's surface in Fig. 2 (b and d). Both CDs@Zr-BDC and CDs@Zr-BPDC showed effective post-synthetic modification and composite production, with particle diameters increasing from 76 nm to 80 nm in the composite.

On the other hand, the SEM images of Zr-BPDC and CDs@Zr-BPDC taken with a scanning electron microscope are shown in Fig. 1(c–d), Zr-BPDC's unique hexagonal shape stands out. As was previously elaborated, there were some minor differences in particle shape when compared to Zr-BDC particles. Zr-metal ions have different chemical affinities when they are placed next to BDC ligands, and this difference can be linked to the use of different ligands (BPDC). The average size of a particle is somewhere between 70 and 80 nm. The encapsulation of carbon dots (CDs) across the Zr-BPDC surface, resulting in the development of a composite structure, is revealed by SEM, as shown in Fig. 1b and d. Hence the electrochemical conductivity of the material is improved due to the integration of carbon dots inside surface of MOFs, which increased surface area of the MOFs. The increased catalytic activity is a direct result of this increase in surface area.

The surface characteristics of Zr-BDC and CDs@Zr-BDC are described using AFM scans, as shown in Fig. 2(a–b). Zr-BDC has a more uniform surface structure than CDs@Zr-BDC, which has more pronounced surface imperfections. Owing to the increased surface roughness of Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) as carbon dot entities are incorporated. Similar to the SEM findings, the AFM findings confirm that Zr-BPDC has a smooth surface structure and that CDs@Zr-BPDC, where carbon dot particles have interacted with the MOF surface, has a higher surface roughness. EDS examination of composition of Zr-BDC and CDs@Zr-BDC, describes presence of elements as 39 %, 26 %, and 34 % of the mass of Zr-BDC are accounted for by carbon (C), oxygen (O), and zirconium (Zr), respectively. Simultaneously, trace impurity elements are detectable, which may be the result of EDS analysis residuals. When carbon dots are incorporated into a composite like CDs@Zr-BDC, the carbon content increases considerably, but the oxygen (O) and zirconium (Zr) percentages fall to 15 % and 29 %, respectively. Zr-BPDC, as shown in Fig. 2(c–d), is composed of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and zirconium (Zr) in the given mass percentages. CDs@Zr-BPDC, on the other hand, is composed of C, N, O, and zirconium (Zr) in the given mass percentages. The incorporation of carbon dots into the CDs@Zr-BPDC composite results in a higher carbon content (37 %) as demonstrated by EDS spectrum. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) adsorption-desorption isotherms for calculating BET surface area of particles. Fig. 2(e–h) shows the BET adsorption-desorption curve for Zr-BDC as well as CDs@Zr-BDC, respectively. Zr-BDC's surface area was a remarkable 57.09 m2/g, whereas, surface area of CDs@Zr-BDC was significantly increased to 73.21 m2/g. The addition of tiny carbon dot particles to CDs@Zr-BDC significantly increased the composite's surface area, which in turn provided an enhanced number of active sites suitable for catalytic sites. Fig. 2(g–j) displays the BET adsorption-desorption patterns for Zr-BPDC and CDs@Zr-BPDC, which are equally enlightening. The BET of the composite CDs@Zr-BPDC was much higher (65.33 m2/g) than that of Zr-BPDC (45.91 m2/g) in this setting. Carbon dot particles attached to the surface of Zr-BPDC explain this mismatch by boosting the material's surface area and, by extension, its catalytic potential.

Powder XRD patterns analyzing structural properties of Zr-BDC and CDs@Zr-BDC are mentioned in Fig. 3 (a–d). Crystal structure of synthesized MOF particles is shown by the presence of XRD peaks at 8°, 10°, 12°, 26°, 31°, 39°, 46°, & 56° in the Zr-BDC spectrum. In contrast, when carbon dots are incorporated into the MOF surface, carbon-specific peaks become visible accompanying a decrease in peak sharpness, indicating reduced crystallinity. XRD patterns for Zr-BPDC as well as CDs@Zr-BPDC are shown in Fig. 3(a–d), where their crystal structures, phases, and particle sizes were analyzed on the same analogy. Peaks in the characteristic XRD pattern may be seen at 8°, 10°, 12°, 16° 26°, 31°, 36° 46°, 56°, and 63°. Crystalline integrity of MOFs is highlighted by the sharpness of these peaks. The peaks in CDs are linked to graphitic domains, exhibiting specific diffraction angles corresponding to aromatic structures [41], while aliphatic structures can contribute to broad peaks or specific angles [42]. The XRD pattern of Zr-MOFs was confirmed with a simulated data in a study conducted by S. Farhadi et al., with main intensity peaks appearing at 8°, confirming the synthesis of the Zr-MOF materials [43]. Crystallite sizes were measured using Scherrer formula, which gave results of 27.5 nm for Zr-BPDC and 32.7 nm for CDs@Zr-BPDC.

To verify successful synthesis of Zr-BDC and composite CDs@Zr-BDC, Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) analysis was evaluated Fig. 3(e–h). Zr-BDC, in particular, showed a prominent peak around 3400 cm−1, which is associated with OH groups present in the ligand itself. Creation of composite structure was greatly aided by presence of hydroxyl groups, as demonstrated by sharp and clearly defined peak [44]. The -COO- ions in the Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) are responsible for peaks at 1637 and 1370 cm−1, which are ascribed to their anti-symmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations, accordingly [45]. Bonding between metal ions and the ligand was confirmed by these peaks. Additional infrared bands, at 2900 cm−1, were seen in spectra that was ascribed towards stretching vibrations of CH bonds [46]. An absorption peak at 490 cm−1, suggestive of ZrO bond formation, was also prominent among numerous peaks in the fingerprint area [47]. The results of the FTIR study performed on Zr-BPDC and CDs@Zr-BPDC are mentioned in Fig. 3(e–h). The FTIR spectrum of Zr-BPDC was very similar to that of Zr-BDC, with some minor differences due to the presence of nitrogen in the benzene ring. Thus, the presence of nitrogen confirmed the structural subtleties associated with this nitrogen-containing moiety by adding a new set of peaks to those already identified.

It is observed that Zr based MOFs and their composites can be assessed for their potential towards OER. Curves of synthesized MOFs and their composites obtained through CV and LSV are shown in Fig. 4 (a–b), while the enlarge CV curve's view of the synthesized products were shown in Fig. 4c. Electrocatalytic potential of all the fabricated substances was evaluated to initiate and then attain the bench mark current density i.e. 10 mAcm−2. Benchmarking current density is crucial for evaluating OER catalyst performance, comparing its effectiveness under specific conditions. It is calculated on a specific overpotential, with 10 mA/cm2 being standard [48]. The onset potential indicated beginning of OER. The fabricated materials showed onset potentials of 1.49, 1.4, 1.48, 1.49 V vs RHE for Zr-BDC/NFE, CDs@Zr-BDC/NFE, Zr-BPDC/NFE and CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE respectively. Similarly, it was observed that Zr-BPDC/NFE require an overpotential of 311 mV to attain bench mark current density (10 mV/cm2), while CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE attained 290 mV. Furthermore, the Zr-BDC/NFE and CDs@Zr-BDC/NFE obtained this current density at 320 mV and 300 mV respectively. These values of overpotential suggested that CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE showed higher current density with lower over potential making it a good electrocatalyst towards OER as compared to Zr-BDC/NFE and CDs@Zr-BDC/NFE and Zr-BPDC/NFE nanohybrid. The present CDs' with MOF composite enhances electrical conductivity by forming conductive routes, facilitating electron flow within the MOF structure [49]. CDs significantly enhance the electrocatalytic activity of Zr-MOFs, enhancing the efficiency and functionality of electrochemical processes like hydrogen evolution reactions [50]. Zr-MOFs and CDs improve the overall electrochemical characteristics through synergistic effects. Better charge transfer kinetics and enhanced electrochemical stability are two examples of this, which improves the composite materials' efficiency and durability in a variety of applications [51].

The current densities of several fabricated electrodes at numerous defined potentials might similarly, be used to obtain identical conclusion. At bench mark of 10 mA cm−2, the histograms of overpotentials of all fabricated electrodes are depicted in Fig. 4(d) correspondingly. When all voltammograms are observed, it became obvious that among all the prepared electrocatalysts, CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE nanostructure showed the minimum value of overpotential to attain bench mark current density and it also showed the maximum current density. Tafel slope curves are basically gained at a potential value where its catalytic kinetics regulates current completely (non-mass-transfer limit) and OER kinetics of the catalyst are usually displayed via minor Tafel slope value. Therefore, in current project, Tafel graphs of fabricated catalysts were drawn which regulated the overall kinetics of the OER in this case. Eq. (1) was fitted on linear section of Tafel graph to induce the Tafel slope.
(1)
ƞ = a + blogj
where j designates the cathodic intercept which is related with exchange current density, where ƞ and b denote overpotential and the Tafel constant, correspondingly. Values of Tafel slopes for all the fabricated electrodes are 91.0 mV/dec for Zr-BDC/NFE, 108 mVdec−1 for Zr-BPDC/NFE, 37 mVdec−1 for CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE, and 53 mVdec−1 for CDs@Zr-BDC/NFE as illustrated in Fig. 4(e) From analysis of these values of Tafel slope, it became obvious that the Tafel slope value of the CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE is obviously lesser when compared with related manufactured substances, demonstrating the enhanced kinetics of the reaction.

The outcomes of Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) of all the synthesized MOFs and their composites indicated the semicircle diameters for these electrocatalysts was decreased in the order Zr-BDC/NFE > CDs@Zr-BDC/NFE > CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE > CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE which clearly indicated that Rct also reduced when CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE nanocomposite was formed as shown in Fig. 3(f). The charge transfer resistance (Rct) and solution resistance (Rs) of the synthesized materials were 5.88, 3.09, 2.75, and 1.71 Ω for Zr-BDC/NFE, CDs@Zr-BDC/NFE, Zr-BPDC/NFE and CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE, respectively. Low value of Rct means minimum resistance and maximum conductance favoring the fast transport of electron between the electrolyte and the interfaces of the electrodes and vice versa [52]. To compare Tafel values and Rct of OER catalysts, measurements must be conducted under identical conditions, including electrolyte, temperature, and electrode configuration, as lower Rct slopes indicate faster kinetics [53]. As anticipated, the fabrication of CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE heterojunction expedited the electrocatalytic activity for the OER due to its lowest values of charge transfer resistance.

The non-Faradaic area of CV technique was used to examine the electrocatalytic active surface area with ECSA for four distinct samples: CDs@Zr-BPDC, Zr-BPDC, CDs@BDC and Zr-BDC, correspondingly are mentioned in Fig. 5 (a–d). Each curve was acquired at a different potential, between 1.15 to 1.25, 1.1 to 1.2, 1.05 to 1.145, and 1.1 to 1.2 V versus RHE. The non-Faradaic zone where no Faradaic processes are taking place and the electrochemical response of double-layer capacitance may be measured. Adjusting surface chemistry via addition of heteroatoms, can improve charge storage performance in materials [54]. Carbon dots enhance conductivity and provide more active charge storage sites in Zr-MOFs, providing effective charge storage due to their large surface area and structural stability [36]. Redox hopping, or the movement of charge-compensating ions, facilitates electrical charge transport in MOF settings, which helps explain these materials' high charge storage capacities [55]. Similar double-layer capacitance (Cdl) graphs for the same samples are mentioned in Fig. 5(a′, b′, c′, d′). Cdl readings of 0.0095 F, 0.0048 F, 0.004 F, and 0.0029 F have been recorded for these samples.

The increased electrochemical performance and more accessible surface area for charge accumulation are both indicators of an increased Cdl value. CDs@Zr-BPDC had the greatest Cdl value of 0.0095 F, suggesting the largest double-layer capacitance among the samples investigated, as shown by the data from the Cdl plots (a’ and b’). Sample CDs@Zr-BDC has a Cdl of 0.0048 F, making it the second highest of the samples. Based on these findings, CDs@Zr-BPDC appeared as the most promising option for electrochemical applications needing effective charge storage and capacitive behavior since it has the most charge storage capacity and, most likely, the biggest accessible surface area for charge accumulation.

Electrocatalytic reactivities of the fabricated MOFs and their composites for splitting of H2O into H2 and O2 under ambient conditions in unit time were also evaluated via fabulous technique known as TOF (Turn over frequency). The value of TOF is bigger, it means electrochemical activity of the fabricated materials is great which also means that its proficiency is also great [56]. Zr-BDC/NFE, CDs@Zr-BDC/NFE, Zr-BPDC/NFE and CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE displayed 0.86 s−1, 0.88 s−1, 0.99 s−1 and 1.78 s−1 turn over frequency values respectively. These TOF values suggested that CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE possessed the highest values as compared to other fabricated materials. Its superior TOF value supported its excellent potential for OER and thus bestowed excellent activity. These modifications improve the accessibility and dispersion of active sites, which is essential for the effectiveness of OER [57]. CDs enhance MOF composites' light absorption and charge separation, enhancing catalytic activity, particularly in electrocatalytic applications, by providing a protective layer for extended stability.

Reason behind significantly higher catalytic activity of CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE heterojunction for OER when compared to all the other prepared materials and other related materials is that the molecular constructions of Zr-MOFs are responsible for contradictory activation rates as revealed above. Zr-MOFs have carbon dots distributed evenly, with functional groups on CDs promoting interaction with MOF structure centers, such as amino groups, through hydrogen or coordination bonds [58]. CDs' homogeneous distribution in Zr-MOFs' pores enhances catalytic activity and stability, inhibiting agglomeration and decreasing effective surface area and catalytic activity. Zr-MOFs' porous nature allows for the encapsulation of CDs within their cavities, thereby stabilizing and shielding them from quenching effects, thus enhancing their luminous properties [59]. CDs significantly enhance the electrocatalytic activity of Zr-MOFs, enabling high electrical conductivity and electron transfer processes, while the MOF structure provides a large surface area for catalytic reactions [60]. The Cell features of Zr-BDC and Zr-BPDC illustrated that in Zr-BPDC, there is a vast separation between its sheets when compared with the gap between the layers of Zr-BDC. In alkaline medium, more and more OH− infiltrated into the gaps between layers of Zr-BPDC, following a faster alteration rate than that of Zr-BDC during the same period of time. This transformation in the above-mentioned alteration rate between Zr-BDC and Zr-BPDC will likely to create oxygen faults when electrochemical activation of CDs@ Zr-BPDC/NFE is carried out.

After being subjected to 1500 successive CV cycles, catalyst displays a persistent reaction characterized by a barely perceptible fluctuation in current density as shown Fig. 6 (a). The efficiency of composite in different pH for OER performance was analyzed to understand the behavior at pH 7 and 4, described in Fig. 6b. The Tafel slope elucidated from this analysis described in Fig. 6c, yielding 71 and 147 mV/dec at pH 7 and 11, respectively. A greater Tafel slope is a result of the slower reaction rate requiring a larger overpotential to obtain the same current density. The atmosphere gets more acidic and there are fewer OH− ions available to take part in reaction at lower pH values. Because of this, reaction is slowed down and more voltage must be applied in order to keep the reaction rate constant as shown in Fig. 6(c).

Fig. 6(d) shows that after 50 h of constant catalysis at an applied potential of 0.75 V, a little drop in current density can be observed. This profile was recorded in a 1.0 M solution of potassium hydroxide (KOH) using a constant current. This section is included to set the CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE heterojunction catalyst's electrochemical response and long-term stability in a more complete comparative framework. Stability of material at various pH values improves efficiency and durability, guarantees safety, and enhanced understanding for the functionality of material under different environmental conditions. These results provide further evidence that CDs@Zr-BPDC/NFE catalyst has greater stability. The structural resilience unique to heterojunctions provides an explanation for this improved stability, which is crucial for many practical uses. The CDs@Zr-BPDC showing comparable stability even changing PH of electrolyte is shown in Fig. 6(d–f). It is most likely because concentration of hydroxide ions (OH−) falls at lower pH values along with increase in Tafel slope. As oxidation reactions depend on concentration of OH− ions, a lower concentration results in slower reaction kinetics.

CDs' carbon-based composition enhances electrical conductivity by forming conductive routes, facilitating electron flow within the MOF structure, making them crucial for electrochemical applications [49]. CDs significantly enhance the electrocatalytic activity of Zr-MOFs, enhancing the efficiency and functionality of electrochemical processes like CO2 reduction and hydrogen evolution reactions [50]. Zr-MOFs and CDs work in concert to improve the overall electrochemical characteristics through synergistic effects. Better charge transfer kinetics and enhanced electrochemical stability are two examples of this, which improves the composite materials' efficiency and durability in a variety of applications [51]. The CDs@Zr-BPDC offers the benefits for the OER, when compared to conventional electrocatalysts are shown in Table.1 .

Hence the improved OER performance of CDs@Zr-BPDC nanocomposite was: (a) In addition to increasing surface area and porosity of Zr-BPDC framework, incorporation of carbon nanoparticles facilitates adsorption and activation of oxygen species across entire OER. (b) The integration of carbon nanoparticles into the MOF could potentially modify its electronic configuration, leading to improved charge transport properties and a diminished overpotential requirement for OER. (c) By fostering synergistic interactions between the two components, this configuration facilitates effective charge transfer. These characteristics underscore the catalytic capability of hybrid MOF-based materials, particularly in the context of energy conversion systems.

In order to offer an auspicious alternative of fossil fuels, MOF based composite was fabricated using solvothermal process. It was aimed at using the catalyst to harness hydrogen (H2) from water (H2O) during the process of OER. In current research study, Zr based MOF and its composites were prepared. Out of these materials, CDs@Zr-BPDC having versatile porosity and immense surface area (73.21 m2/g), was employed to deal the imminent problems of global warming and energy crisis. The incorporation of CDs into the Zr-MOF boosted electrocatalytic activity by enhancing the surface area and generating a synergistic effect between MOF and CDs. These attributes enabled CDs@Zr-BPDC to initiate the sluggish OER at a small onset potential of 1.45 V vs RHE. It remained wonderful in achieving 10 mA cm−2 at unusually small overpotential close to 290 mV. It exhibited a minimal Tafel value of 37 mV/dec which further approved its wonderful capability to allow charge transfer quickly at interface of electrode-electrolyte. Consequently, it displayed amazingly a Rct as small as 1.2 Ω. It illustrated a vast electrochemical active surface of 237.5 cm2. All these characteristics favored CDs@Zr-BPDC to be a potential contestant in a marathon for searching a probable alternative of fossil fuel.
